facts.text: Reggae music originated in Jamaica in the late 1960s, evolving from earlier genres like ska and rocksteady.
The word 'reggae' first appeared in the 1968 single 'Do the Reggay' by Toots and the Maytals.
Bob Marley is widely regarded as the most iconic reggae musician of all time.
The one drop rhythm, characterized by an emphasis on the third beat of each measure, is a defining feature of roots reggae.
The Wailers, consisting of Bob Marley, Peter Tosh, and Bunny Wailer, formed in 1963 in Trench Town, Kingston.
Studio One, founded by Clement 'Coxsone' Dodd, is often called the 'Motown of Jamaica.'
Lee 'Scratch' Perry is considered one of the most innovative music producers in history.
King Tubby is credited as a pioneer of dub music, a subgenre that strips tracks down to their rhythm and bass.
Island Records, founded by Chris Blackwell, was instrumental in bringing reggae to international audiences.
Trojan Records, founded in 1968, became one of the most important labels for distributing reggae in the UK.
Bob Marley's album 'Exodus' was named Album of the Century by Time magazine in 1999.
Rastafari is a spiritual movement that has deeply influenced reggae music's themes of liberation and spirituality.
Reggae's skank guitar technique involves playing short, choppy chords on the off-beat.
Burning Spear, born Winston Rodney, is one of the most respected roots reggae artists.
Peter Tosh released his landmark solo album 'Equal Rights' in 1977, advocating for human rights and cannabis legalization.
Jimmy Cliff starred in the 1972 film 'The Harder They Come,' which introduced reggae to a global audience.
Sound systems, mobile discotheques playing recorded music, have been central to Jamaican music culture since the 1950s.
Dub music was created by remixing existing reggae tracks, removing vocals, and emphasizing drums and bass.
The riddim is a recurring instrumental backing track in reggae and dancehall over which multiple artists record vocals.
Bob Marley was born on February 6, 1945, in Nine Mile, Saint Ann Parish, Jamaica.
Ska music preceded reggae and was characterized by a walking bass line and off-beat rhythm.
Rocksteady emerged around 1966 as a slower, more soulful evolution of ska.
Dancehall originated in the late 1970s as a more uptempo, DJ-driven offshoot of reggae.
Lovers rock is a subgenre of reggae that emerged in the UK in the mid-1970s, featuring romantic themes.
Bob Marley and the Wailers' 'Catch a Fire' (1973) was the first reggae album marketed to a rock audience.
The Nyabinghi drumming tradition of Rastafari directly influenced reggae's rhythmic patterns.
Bob Marley survived an assassination attempt at his home in Kingston on December 3, 1976.
Toots Hibbert of Toots and the Maytals was one of the first artists to use the word 'reggae' in a song title.
The electric bass guitar replaced the upright bass in Jamaican music during the transition from ska to rocksteady.
Bob Marley's 'One Love' was adopted as a universal anthem of peace and unity.
The Skatalites were Jamaica's premier ska band and backed many recordings at Studio One in the 1960s.
Reggae Sunsplash, one of the first major reggae festivals, debuted in Montego Bay in 1978.
Prince Buster was a pioneering figure in Jamaican ska and early reggae music.
Desmond Dekker's 'Israelites' was the first reggae song to reach number one on the UK Singles Chart in 1969.
Bob Marley died on May 11, 1981, at the age of 36 from melanoma.
Trench Town, a neighborhood in Kingston, Jamaica, is often called the birthplace of reggae.
The Rastafarian colors of red, gold, and green are frequently associated with reggae music and culture.
Sly Dunbar and Robbie Shakespeare are considered the most prolific rhythm section in reggae history.
Bob Marley's 'Legend' (1984) is the best-selling reggae album of all time, with over 30 million copies sold.
The Melodians' 'Rivers of Babylon' became an international hit after appearing in 'The Harder They Come' soundtrack.
Jamaican DJs, also called toasters, developed a style of talking over instrumental riddims that influenced hip-hop.
King Tubby's studio on Dromilly Avenue in Kingston was a laboratory for dub experimentation.
Chris Blackwell signed Bob Marley and the Wailers to Island Records in 1972, changing reggae's global trajectory.
The one drop drum pattern was popularized by Carlton Barrett, the drummer for Bob Marley and the Wailers.
Reggae was inscribed on UNESCO's Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity in 2018.
Steel Pulse, from Birmingham, England, became one of the most important UK reggae bands.
Big Youth was one of the first dancehall DJs to achieve mainstream success in the 1970s.
Bob Marley's 'Redemption Song' is one of the most covered songs in music history.
Yellowman became one of dancehall's first mainstream stars in the early 1980s.
Reggae music often incorporates themes of social justice, resistance to oppression, and Pan-Africanism.
Lee 'Scratch' Perry's Black Ark Studio, operational from 1973 to 1979, produced some of reggae's most influential recordings.
Third World is one of the longest-running reggae bands, blending reggae with R&B, funk, and pop.
The Congos' album 'Heart of the Congos,' produced by Lee Perry, is considered a masterpiece of roots reggae.
Gregory Isaacs was nicknamed the 'Cool Ruler' for his smooth vocal style in lovers rock and roots reggae.
Aswad was a pioneering British reggae band formed in Ladbroke Grove, London, in 1975.
Inner Circle won a Grammy for Best Reggae Album in 1994 for 'Bad Boys.'
Shaggy's album 'Hot Shot' reached number one on the Billboard 200 in 2001.
Sean Paul's 'Dutty Rock' won the Grammy for Best Reggae Album in 2004.
Damian 'Jr. Gong' Marley, Bob Marley's youngest son, won a Grammy for 'Welcome to Jamrock' in 2006.
Ziggy Marley has won eight Grammy Awards, continuing his father's musical legacy.
The first Reggae Grammy Award was presented in 1985 to Black Uhuru for their album 'Anthem.'
Eric Donaldson's 'Cherry Oh Baby' won the Jamaica Festival Song Competition in 1971 and became a reggae classic.
Barrington Levy's 'Here I Come' is one of the most recognizable dancehall tracks of the 1980s.
The Abyssinians' 'Satta Massagana' is one of the greatest roots reggae songs ever recorded.
Sister Nancy's 'Bam Bam' is considered a classic of early dancehall, released in 1982.
DJ Kool Herc, a Jamaican-American, is credited with originating hip-hop in the Bronx, drawing from Jamaican sound system culture.
Clement 'Coxsone' Dodd founded Studio One in 1963, which became the most important recording studio in Jamaica.
Duke Reid operated the Treasure Isle studio, a rival to Studio One that produced many classic ska and rocksteady tracks.
The 'Sleng Teng' riddim, produced by King Jammy in 1985, revolutionized dancehall by using digital instrumentation.
Beenie Man and Bounty Killer had one of dancehall's most famous rivalries throughout the 1990s.
Buju Banton's album 'Til Shiloh' marked his transition from dancehall to roots reggae in 1995.
Chronixx is one of the leading artists in the reggae revival movement that emerged in the 2010s.
Protoje, a Jamaican singer-songwriter, has been at the forefront of the modern reggae revival.
Koffee became the youngest artist and first woman to win the Grammy for Best Reggae Album in 2020.
Jamaica has a population of approximately 2.8 million but has had an outsized influence on global music.
The 'Stalag 17' riddim is one of the most versioned riddims in reggae and dancehall history.
Marcia Griffiths, a member of the I-Threes, is one of reggae's most celebrated female artists.
The I-Threes were Bob Marley's backing vocal group, consisting of Rita Marley, Marcia Griffiths, and Judy Mowatt.
Judy Mowatt was the first female reggae artist to be nominated for a Grammy Award.
Bob Marley performed at the Zimbabwe Independence Day celebration on April 17, 1980.
The One Love Peace Concert held on April 22, 1978, saw Bob Marley unite political rivals Michael Manley and Edward Seaga on stage.
Reggae bass players often use a technique called 'dropping' where the bass plays on beats one and three.
Studio One's house band, the Soul Brothers, later renamed the Sound Dimension, backed hundreds of recordings.
The Specials and Madness led the 2 Tone ska revival movement in late 1970s Britain, blending ska with punk energy.
The Police incorporated reggae rhythms into rock music, most notably on 'Roxanne' and 'Walking on the Moon.'
The Clash recorded at Channel One Studio in Kingston and integrated reggae into their punk sound.
Eric Clapton's cover of Bob Marley's 'I Shot the Sheriff' reached number one in the US in 1974.
Bob Marley's 'No Woman, No Cry' was recorded live at the Lyceum Theatre in London in 1975.
Johnny Nash's 'I Can See Clearly Now,' recorded in Jamaica, was a number-one hit in the US in 1972.
Channel One Studio, run by the Hookim brothers, was a rival to Studio One and produced many classic roots reggae recordings.
The Roots Radics were one of the most important backing bands in the transition from roots reggae to early dancehall.
Scientist, a protege of King Tubby, became one of the most prominent dub engineers of the early 1980s.
Alpha Boys' School in Kingston trained many of the musicians who shaped ska and reggae.
Don Drummond, a trombonist from Alpha Boys' School, was one of the founding members of the Skatalites.
Derrick Morgan was one of the most prolific artists of the ska era, recording hundreds of singles.
Laurel Aitken is considered the 'Godfather of Ska' and was one of the first Jamaican artists to record.
Alton Ellis is known as the 'Godfather of Rocksteady' for his pivotal role in the genre.
The Heptones were one of the most popular vocal trios during the rocksteady era.
Ken Boothe's cover of 'Everything I Own' reached number one in the UK in 1974.
The Ethiopians' 'Train to Skaville' is one of the definitive rocksteady tracks.
Bob Marley's first single, 'Judge Not,' was recorded in 1962 when he was just 17 years old.
Joe Higgs, known as the 'Father of Reggae,' mentored Bob Marley, Peter Tosh, and Bunny Wailer in Trench Town.
Peter Tosh was murdered during a home invasion in Kingston on September 11, 1987.
Bunny Wailer passed away on March 2, 2021, at the age of 73.
Toots Hibbert passed away on September 11, 2020, shortly after releasing 'Got to Be Tough.'
The Upsetters were Lee 'Scratch' Perry's backing band, known for their innovative studio work.
Lee Perry's Black Ark Studio was destroyed by fire in 1979 under mysterious circumstances.
King Tubby was born Osbourne Ruddock and was originally a radio repairman before becoming a sound engineer.
King Tubby was shot and killed outside his home in Kingston on February 6, 1989.
Dub poetry, pioneered by artists like Linton Kwesi Johnson and Mutabaruka, combines spoken word with reggae rhythms.
Linton Kwesi Johnson is a Jamaican-British poet whose work addresses the experience of Black Britons.
The 'Real Rock' riddim, originally by Sound Dimension, is one of the most recycled riddims in reggae history.
Jacob Miller was the lead singer of Inner Circle before his death in a car accident in 1980.
Culture, led by Joseph Hill, recorded the seminal roots reggae album 'Two Sevens Clash' in 1977.
Black Uhuru became one of the most internationally successful reggae groups in the early 1980s.
Michael Rose was the original lead vocalist of Black Uhuru during their most acclaimed period.
The Gladiators, led by Albert Griffiths, were one of the finest roots reggae groups of the 1970s.
Luciano, known as 'The Messenger,' is renowned for his spiritual and conscious reggae lyrics.
Sizzla Kalonji is one of the most prolific recording artists in reggae, with over 50 albums.
Capleton, known as 'The Fireman,' is a leading figure in conscious dancehall reggae.
Morgan Heritage is a family group and part of the Marley-influenced roots reggae tradition.
Tarrus Riley's 'She's Royal' is one of the most popular modern reggae love songs.
Maxi Priest was the first reggae artist to have a number-one hit on the US Billboard Hot 100 with 'Close to You' in 1990.
Shabba Ranks brought dancehall to mainstream American audiences in the early 1990s.
Super Cat, also known as 'Wild Apache,' was one of dancehall's biggest stars in the late 1980s and early 1990s.
Ini Kamoze's 'Here Comes the Hotstepper' reached number one on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1994.
Sean Paul's 'Get Busy' reached number one on the Billboard Hot 100 in 2003.
Shaggy's 'It Wasn't Me' was a number-one hit in multiple countries in 2001.
Popcaan, a protege of Vybz Kartel, has become one of dancehall's biggest international stars.
Spice, known as the 'Queen of Dancehall,' is one of the genre's most prominent female artists.
Patra was one of the first female dancehall artists to achieve international success in the 1990s.
VP Records, founded by Vincent and Patricia Chin, is one of the largest distributors of reggae music.
Greensleeves Records, founded in London in 1977, was a major label for reggae and dancehall in the UK.
Joe Gibbs was a prolific reggae producer whose studio rivaled Studio One and Channel One.
Robbie Shakespeare passed away on December 8, 2021.
The Wailing Souls have been active since the 1960s and are one of reggae's longest-running groups.
Justin Hinds and the Dominoes were among the most popular artists during the ska era in Jamaica.
Marcus Garvey, the Jamaican national hero, is frequently referenced in reggae music as a prophet of repatriation.
Haile Selassie I of Ethiopia is considered the returned messiah in Rastafari and is revered in many reggae songs.
Haile Selassie visited Jamaica on April 21, 1966, a date now celebrated as Grounation Day by Rastafarians.
Bob Marley's birthday, February 6, is celebrated annually in Jamaica as Bob Marley Day.
Tenor Saw's 'Ring the Alarm' is one of the most recognizable early digital dancehall tracks.
Reggae en Espanol is popular throughout Latin America, with strong scenes in Argentina, Chile, and Mexico.
Alpha Blondy from Ivory Coast is one of the most prominent reggae artists from Africa.
Lucky Dube was murdered during a carjacking in Johannesburg on October 18, 2007.
Tiken Jah Fakoly from Ivory Coast uses reggae as a vehicle for political activism in West Africa.
Reggae has a strong following in Japan, with artists like Pushim and the Mighty Crown sound system.
Mighty Crown is a Japanese sound system that won the World Clash competition in 1999.
Reggae is hugely popular in Hawaii, where it has blended with local music to create 'Jawaiian' music.
The Rototom Sunsplash Festival in Spain is one of the largest reggae festivals in Europe.
Summerjam in Cologne, Germany, is one of Europe's biggest reggae and dancehall festivals.
International Reggae Day is celebrated annually on July 1.
New Zealand has a vibrant reggae scene influenced by Polynesian culture, with artists like Katchafire.
Gentleman, a German reggae artist, records and performs primarily in Jamaican Patois.
The term 'irie' is a Jamaican Patois word meaning good, positive, or feeling alright.
Dreadlocks are closely associated with Rastafari culture and many reggae musicians.
The Lion of Judah is an important symbol in Rastafari and appears frequently in reggae imagery.
Nyabinghi is one of the main orders of Rastafari and is known for its drumming ceremonies.
Count Ossie was a Nyabinghi drummer who helped integrate Rastafarian rhythms into popular Jamaican music.
Count Ossie and the Mystic Revelation of Rastafari released the groundbreaking album 'Grounation' in 1973.
Bob Marley has eleven officially recognized children, many of whom became musicians.
Stephen Marley has won multiple Grammy Awards as both an artist and producer.
Skip Marley, Bob Marley's grandson, collaborated with Katy Perry on the hit 'Chained to the Rhythm.'
YG Marley, another grandson of Bob Marley, went viral with 'Praise Jah in the Moonlight' in 2023.
The Bob Marley Museum in Kingston is located at 56 Hope Road, the former home and studio of the legend.
Tuff Gong International is a Jamaican record label founded by Bob Marley in 1970.
Peter Tosh's 'Legalize It' (1976) was one of the first reggae albums to explicitly advocate marijuana legalization.
Jimmy Cliff was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2010.
Jimmy Cliff's 'Many Rivers to Cross' is considered one of the greatest songs in Jamaican music.
The soundtrack to 'The Harder They Come' was one of the best-selling reggae albums in the US.
Lee Perry was awarded the Order of Distinction by the Jamaican government for his contributions to music.
Lee Perry passed away on August 29, 2021, at the age of 85.
Perry Henzell directed 'The Harder They Come' starring Jimmy Cliff, which premiered in 1972.
Sound system culture in Jamaica traces back to the 1950s when operators like Tom the Great Sebastian pioneered outdoor dances.
Sir Coxsone's Downbeat was one of Jamaica's most famous sound systems in the 1950s and 1960s.
Duke Reid's Trojan sound system was a fierce rival to Coxsone Dodd's Downbeat.
Dub plates, exclusive acetate recordings, are used by sound systems to gain a competitive edge.
Stone Love Movement, founded in 1972, is one of Jamaica's most famous and enduring sound systems.
Jah Shaka is a legendary UK-based sound system operator known for powerful dub sessions.
David Rodigan is a British DJ and musicologist who has championed reggae on UK radio since 1978.
The Notting Hill Carnival in London features significant reggae and sound system culture.
Birmingham, England, has been a major center for UK reggae, producing bands like Steel Pulse and UB40.
Musical Youth's 'Pass the Dutchie' reached number one in the UK in 1982 and was a global hit.
Rebelution is one of the most popular American reggae bands, originating from Santa Barbara, California.
Groundation is an American roots reggae band known for their jazz-influenced instrumentation.
Eek-A-Mouse is known for his unusual 'singjay' style, blending singing with DJ toasting.
Wayne Smith's 'Under Mi Sleng Teng' (1985) is widely considered the first fully digital reggae hit.
The Casio MT-40 keyboard was used to create the 'Sleng Teng' riddim that changed dancehall forever.
Sugar Minott founded the Youth Promotion label to give unsigned artists a chance to record.
Sugar Minott is credited with helping to bridge the gap between roots reggae and early dancehall.
Mikey Dread was a pioneering radio DJ and producer who hosted 'Dread at the Controls' on JBC Radio.
Mikey Dread collaborated with The Clash on their album 'Sandinista!' in 1980.
Junior Murvin's 'Police and Thieves,' produced by Lee Perry, became an anthem of social unrest in 1976.
Mutabaruka is a Jamaican dub poet and radio host known for his politically charged performances.
The Ital diet, practiced by many Rastafarians, avoids processed food and often excludes meat.
The term 'Babylon' in reggae lyrics refers to oppressive systems of Western society.
The concept of 'Zion' in reggae represents the promised land or a state of spiritual freedom.
The Twelve Tribes of Israel is one of the largest Rastafarian organizations, and Bob Marley was a member.
Bunny Wailer's 'Blackheart Man' (1976) is considered one of the greatest reggae albums ever made.
The Mighty Diamonds are a Jamaican harmony trio known for their album 'Right Time' (1976).
Ijahman Levi is a Jamaican roots reggae artist known for his spiritual and mystical lyrics.
Pablo Moses is a roots reggae artist known for his album 'Revolutionary Dream' (1975).
Yabby You, born Vivian Jackson, was a roots reggae singer and producer known for his prophetic lyrics.
Earth, Wind & Fire and Stevie Wonder were influenced by reggae rhythms in some of their recordings.
The Rolling Stones recorded at Dynamic Sounds studio in Kingston, Jamaica, in 1972.
Keith Richards of the Rolling Stones is a well-known reggae enthusiast and friend of many Jamaican musicians.
Blondie's 'The Tide Is High' was originally a rocksteady song by the Paragons from 1967.
Led Zeppelin's 'D'yer Mak'er' was influenced by reggae rhythms, though it divided fans and purists.
No Doubt blended ska and reggae influences into their sound, especially on 'Tragic Kingdom.'
Rihanna, from Barbados, has incorporated dancehall and reggae influences into many of her hit songs.
Major Lazer, a project by Diplo, has drawn heavily from dancehall and reggae music.
The 'dembow' rhythm pattern used in reggaeton and Latin trap originates from Jamaican dancehall.
Shabba Ranks won back-to-back Grammy Awards for Best Reggae Album in 1992 and 1993.
Diana King's 'Shy Guy' was a huge dancehall crossover hit featured in the film 'Bad Boys' (1995).
Chaka Demus and Pliers had a number-one UK hit with 'Twist and Shout' in 1993.
The original 'Oh Carolina' was recorded by the Folkes Brothers in 1959, one of the earliest Jamaican recordings.
Dawn Penn's 'You Don't Love Me (No, No, No)' was a massive international hit when re-released in 1994.
Garnett Silk was a rising roots reggae star before his tragic death in a fire on December 9, 1994.
Anthony B is a Jamaican singjay known for his fiery, Rastafarian-inspired performances.
Richie Spice is a roots reggae artist known for hits like 'Earth A Run Red.'
Etana is one of the most prominent female reggae artists of the 21st century.
Queen Ifrica is a Jamaican reggae singer known for her powerful voice and conscious lyrics.
Jah9, a Jamaican singer and yoga instructor, is a leading figure in the modern roots reggae revival.
Kabaka Pyramid won the Grammy for Best Reggae Album in 2023 for 'The Kalling.'
Lila Ike is a Jamaican singer associated with the In.Digg.Nation collective and the reggae revival.
Tanya Stephens is a Jamaican singer known for outspoken lyrics on gender and social issues.
The Wailers' 'Simmer Down,' recorded at Studio One in 1963, was their first number-one hit in Jamaica.
Bob Marley's 'Buffalo Soldier' was released posthumously in 1983 and became a worldwide hit.
'Natty Dread' (1974) was the first Bob Marley and the Wailers album without Peter Tosh and Bunny Wailer.
Bob Marley received the United Nations Peace Medal of the Third World in 1978.
The Smile Jamaica Concert, organized by Bob Marley, was held on December 5, 1976.
Bob Marley played a famous concert at the Rainbow Theatre in London on June 1, 1977.
'Rastaman Vibration' (1976) was the first Bob Marley album to crack the US Top 10 on the Billboard 200.
'Kaya' (1978) took a softer, more introspective approach compared to Marley's earlier political albums.
'Survival' (1979) is considered Bob Marley's most overtly Pan-Africanist album.
'Uprising' (1980) was the last album released by Bob Marley during his lifetime.
The song 'Three Little Birds' by Bob Marley is one of his most beloved and frequently covered songs.
'Could You Be Loved' was one of Bob Marley's most dance-oriented tracks, blending reggae with disco.
'Jamming' was released in 1977 and became one of Bob Marley's most popular songs worldwide.
'Get Up, Stand Up,' co-written by Bob Marley and Peter Tosh, became a human rights anthem.
'War' by Bob Marley was based on a speech by Haile Selassie I delivered to the United Nations in 1963.
The cover art of 'Catch a Fire' originally came in a Zippo lighter-shaped sleeve, one of the most distinctive album packages.
Peter Tosh's 'Bush Doctor' (1978) featured a duet with Mick Jagger on the track 'Don't Look Back.'
Jimmy Cliff's 'Wonderful World, Beautiful People' was an early international reggae hit in 1969.
Jimmy Cliff converted to Islam in the early 1970s and took the name El-Hadj Naim Bachir.
Toots and the Maytals' 'Pressure Drop' was featured in 'The Harder They Come' soundtrack.
Toots and the Maytals blended reggae with soul, gospel, and R&B, creating a unique sound.
Desmond Dekker's '007 (Shanty Town)' was one of the first songs to reference the 'rude boy' subculture.
The 'rude boy' subculture of 1960s Jamaica influenced both ska and early reggae music.
Treasure Isle Records, Duke Reid's label, produced many of the most important rocksteady recordings.
Harry J Studio in Kingston has hosted recording sessions for many international and Jamaican artists.
The Dynamic Sounds studio in Kingston was one of the first multi-track recording studios in the Caribbean.
Randy's Studio 17, run by the Chin family, was an important Kingston studio during the 1970s.
King Tubby's techniques of reverb, delay, and echo informed electronic music production globally.
The dub remix style pioneered by King Tubby and Lee Perry is considered a precursor to modern remix culture.
Augustus Pablo's 'King Tubby Meets Rockers Uptown' (1976) is often cited as the greatest dub album ever.
The 'Far East' riddim, produced by Augustus Pablo, blended Eastern musical influences with reggae.
Easy Star All-Stars' 'Dub Side of the Moon' is a reggae reimagining of Pink Floyd's 'The Dark Side of the Moon.'
The Aggrovators were a studio band assembled by Bunny Lee that played on dozens of classic reggae tracks.
Bunny 'Striker' Lee is one of Jamaica's most prolific record producers, active since the 1960s.
Jack Ruby produced Burning Spear's seminal 'Marcus Garvey' album.
Gussie Clarke is a Jamaican producer known for blending roots reggae with digital production in the 1980s.
Dave Kelly, founder of the Madhouse label, produced many of the biggest dancehall hits of the 1990s.
Stephen McGregor, known as 'Di Genius,' is one of the most prolific dancehall producers of the 21st century.
The Penthouse label, run by Donovan Germain, produced numerous dancehall hits in the 1990s.
The Taxi Gang, led by Sly and Robbie, is one of the most in-demand backing bands in reggae history.
Carlton Barrett pioneered the one drop rhythm and was a master of subtle percussion.
Aston 'Family Man' Barrett has been called the most important bass player in reggae history.
Carlton Barrett was shot and killed outside his home in Kingston on April 17, 1987.
The Marley Coffee brand is managed by Rohan Marley, Bob Marley's son.
The Bob Marley biopic 'One Love' was released in February 2024, starring Kingsley Ben-Adir.
The 'Bam Bam' riddim has been versioned by dozens of artists since Sister Nancy's original.
Deejaying in Jamaica involves chanting or talking over riddims, distinct from the American DJ who selects records.
U-Roy is credited as the originator of the DJ style of toasting over records in Jamaica.
I-Roy was one of the most influential DJs of the 1970s, known for his witty and rapid-fire delivery.
Brigadier Jerry was a leading dancehall DJ associated with the Twelve Tribes of Israel.
Charlie Chaplin (the Jamaican DJ, not the actor) was a popular dancehall artist in the 1980s.
Ninjaman, known as 'The Don Gorgon,' was one of the most controversial and influential dancehall DJs.
Bounty Killer, known as the 'Warlord,' has been one of dancehall's biggest stars since the 1990s.
Beenie Man's 'Who Am I' was a massive international dancehall hit in 1998.
Beenie Man won the Grammy for Best Reggae Album in 2001 for 'Art and Life.'
Skillibeng blends dancehall with hip-hop and trap, representing a new generation of Jamaican artists.
The 'Diwali' riddim (2003) featured Sean Paul and Bounty Killer and was one of the biggest riddims ever.
VP Records' 'Strictly the Best' compilation series has documented reggae and dancehall hits since 1991.
Jamaica gained independence from Britain on August 6, 1962, and music was central to national identity.
Sting is Jamaica's biggest annual stage show for dancehall, held every Boxing Day.
Rebel Salute, founded by Tony Rebel, is an annual roots reggae festival held in Jamaica every January.
Reggae Sumfest in Montego Bay is one of the Caribbean's biggest annual music festivals.
Michael Manley, Jamaica's Prime Minister in the 1970s, was closely associated with reggae and Bob Marley.
Edward Seaga had ties to the music industry, having produced records at Studio One in the 1960s.
Trench Town Culture Yard is a museum and community center in the neighborhood where Bob Marley grew up.
'Versions' or 'version sides' are instrumental B-sides of reggae singles, a tradition that gave rise to dub.
Reggae's influence can be heard in the music of artists as diverse as The Fugees, Snoop Dogg, and Ed Sheeran.
Lauryn Hill's 'The Miseducation of Lauryn Hill' was influenced by reggae and partly recorded in Jamaica.
Bob Marley was posthumously inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 1994.
The Grammy Award for Best Reggae Album has been presented annually since 1985.
Bad Brains, from Washington D.C., were pioneers in blending hardcore punk with reggae.
Grace Jones, a Jamaican-American model and singer, incorporated reggae and dub into her new wave music.
Bristol's trip-hop scene, including Massive Attack and Portishead, drew heavily from dub and reggae.
Shaggy served in the US Marine Corps during the Gulf War before launching his music career.
Damian Marley's 'Welcome to Jamrock' was one of the biggest reggae crossover hits of the 2000s.
Damian Marley and Nas collaborated on the album 'Distant Relatives' in 2010.
Ghetto Youths International is a record label run by Damian, Stephen, and Julian Marley.
The term 'steppers' refers to a reggae rhythm where the bass drum plays on every beat.
The Hammond organ and clavinet are frequently used keyboard instruments in classic reggae recordings.
Reggae's tempo typically ranges from 60 to 90 beats per minute, giving it a relaxed feel.
Bob Marley's 'Zimbabwe' became the de facto anthem of the Zimbabwean independence movement.
UB40's cover of Neil Diamond's 'Red Red Wine' became a global number-one hit in 1988.
Steel Pulse's debut album 'Handsworth Revolution' (1978) addressed racism and inequality in Britain.
Janet Kay's 'Silly Games' (1979) was a number-two UK hit and a definitive lovers rock track.
Carroll Thompson is known as the 'Queen of Lovers Rock' for her smooth vocal stylings.
The Saxon Sound System in London launched the careers of Maxi Priest, Smiley Culture, and Tippa Irie.
Smiley Culture's 'Cockney Translation' cleverly blended Jamaican Patois with London cockney slang.
Jungle music, which emerged in the UK in the early 1990s, directly incorporated reggae and dancehall samples.
Dubstep, which emerged in South London in the early 2000s, was heavily influenced by dub reggae.
Reggae has been performed in over 40 languages, demonstrating its truly global appeal.
Bob Marley's 'Legend' compilation has spent more than 750 weeks on the Billboard 200 chart.
'Catch a Fire' was remixed by Chris Blackwell to add rock guitar overdubs for wider appeal.
Bob Marley's 'Natural Mystic' opens 'Exodus' with one of reggae's most atmospheric introductions.
Don Carlos, formerly of Black Uhuru, had a successful solo career in roots reggae.
Junior Reid's 'One Blood' became a reggae anthem and has been widely sampled in hip-hop.
Buju Banton's 'Untold Stories' is considered one of the most powerful songs in reggae.
Buju Banton won the Grammy for Best Reggae Album in 2011 for 'Before the Dawn.'
Sean Paul has sold over 20 million albums worldwide, making him one of dancehall's best-selling artists.
Burning Spear was born on March 1, 1945, in Saint Ann's Bay, the same parish as Bob Marley.
'Pressure Drop' by Toots and the Maytals has been covered by The Clash, The Specials, and Keith Richards.
Bob Marley's 'Stir It Up' was originally recorded in 1967 but became a hit when re-recorded in 1972.
The 'Punky Reggae Party' single was a declaration of solidarity between punk and reggae in 1977.
'Confrontation' (1983) was a posthumous Bob Marley album containing previously unreleased material.
Bob Marley's mausoleum is located in Nine Mile, Saint Ann Parish, Jamaica, his birthplace.
Cedella Marley, Bob's daughter, is a singer, author, and fashion designer who heads the Marley brand.
Frankie Paul, known as the 'Jamaican Stevie Wonder,' was one of the most prolific vocalists of the 1980s.
Matisyahu, an American artist, blended reggae with Jewish themes and achieved mainstream success.
Slightly Stoopid blends reggae with rock, blues, and hip-hop and are major figures in the US reggae scene.
Gondwana is one of the most popular reggae bands in Chile and Latin America.
General Levy's 'Incredible' was a massive jungle and reggae crossover hit in the UK in 1994.
Pinnacle was one of the first Rastafarian communities in Jamaica, founded by Leonard Howell in the 1940s.
Shabba Ranks' 'Mr. Loverman' was a crossover dancehall hit reaching the top 5 in the UK and US.
Sanchez is one of the most respected lovers rock and dancehall vocalists from Jamaica.
Niney the Observer was a hitmaker producer who worked at both Studio One and Channel One.
Bobby Digital, born Robert Dixon, helped define the digital dancehall sound as a producer.
Mixing Lab studio, owned by King Jammy, was where the digital dancehall revolution took place.
The horn section in early reggae was essential, with trombones, trumpets, and saxophones adding depth.
The 'bubble' organ pattern in reggae involves repeating a rhythmic chord pattern on the off-beat.
Clarendon Parish in Jamaica has produced notable reggae artists including Barrington Levy and Freddie McGregor.
Rvssian is a Jamaican producer who has worked with international artists like Kanye West.
The Jamaica Festival Song Competition, established in 1966, was an important showcase for reggae talent.
Half Pint's 'Greetings' was one of the biggest reggae hits of 1986.
Political violence in Kingston during the 1970s and 1980s deeply influenced reggae and dancehall lyrics.
The 'Riddim Twin' phenomenon means two songs are often released simultaneously on the same instrumental.
Mr. Vegas' 'Heads High' is one of the most iconic dancehall tracks of the late 1990s.
T.O.K. is a dancehall group from Kingston known for their harmonies and party anthems.
Mento is considered a precursor to ska and reggae, and the Jolly Boys are one of its oldest groups.
Jean 'Binta' Breeze was a Jamaican dub poet who brought the art form to theatrical stages worldwide.
The Twinkle Brothers are a Jamaican roots reggae band that has been recording since the late 1960s.
The Itals are a roots reggae vocal trio known for their close harmonies.
Bass Culture sound system events in London helped popularize reggae in the UK in the 1970s and 1980s.
Derrick Harriott was both a performer and producer who helped shape the rocksteady sound.
Cutty Ranks brought a distinctive gruff vocal style to dancehall in the late 1980s.
Josey Wales was one of the most popular dancehall DJs of the early 1980s.
Ranking Joe was a popular dancehall DJ who performed on many classic sound system sessions.
Drum and bass, an evolution of jungle, continues to draw from dub and reggae bassline traditions.
'Rockers' (1978) is a Jamaican film that showcases the real reggae music scene of the late 1970s.
LKJ released 'Dread Beat an' Blood' in 1978, one of the first dub poetry albums.
10cc's 'Dreadlock Holiday' (1978) was a pop hit inspired by reggae that reached number one in the UK.
Julian Marley, son of Bob Marley, has earned Grammy nominations for his reggae albums.
Ky-Mani Marley, Bob Marley's son, has released several reggae albums and also acted in films.
Dennis Brown passed away on July 1, 1999, and his death was mourned across the reggae world.
Cedella Booker, Bob Marley's mother, wrote a memoir about raising the reggae icon.
The documentary 'Marley' (2012) directed by Kevin Macdonald chronicles Bob Marley's life and legacy.
Bob Marley's 'Is This Love' remains one of the most played reggae love songs of all time.

Katchafire is a New Zealand roots reggae band that has achieved international recognition.
The concept of 'livity' in Rastafari refers to living a righteous and natural lifestyle.
The Jolly Boys are one of the oldest mento groups still performing in Jamaica.
Reggae bass players often use flatwound strings to achieve a deep, rounded tone.
The film "Countryman" (1982) featured a soundtrack with music by Bob Marley and Toots and the Maytals.
Naomi Cowan is a Canadian-Jamaican artist who blends pop, dancehall, and reggae influences.
House of Marley produces audio equipment inspired by Bob Marley's legacy, using sustainable materials.
The Wailers Band continued touring extensively after Bob Marley's death, keeping the Wailers legacy alive.
